title: Chapter 25: The Extraction
content: The basement stairs had not improved with repetition. Each step groaned under Jane's weight, the wood warped from decades of Loire Valley humidity, and the air thickened as she descended — damp stone, old wool, the chemical sweetness of fabric sizing. She had learned to breathe through her mouth down here. It helped with the smell, if not the dread.

Grace was waiting at the bottom, keys in hand, her broad face unreadable as always. She unlocked the door without a word, and Jane stepped through into the amber glow of Bertha Mason's underground kingdom.

Bertha sat at her worktable beneath the single hanging lamp, silver shears catching the light as she trimmed a seam. The punk tuxedo jacket lay spread before her like a dissected animal, its lining exposed, pins bristling along one lapel. She didn't look up when Jane entered.
"It's done," Jane said.
The shears paused. Bertha's dark eyes lifted, sharp as the blades in her hand. "All of it?"

"Richard verified the shell companies this morning. Seven entities funneling Maison Rochester's revenue through Luxembourg and the Caymans. The paper trail is complete — he's confident the financial regulators will take it seriously once it's filed."
Bertha set the shears down with a soft click. "And Helen?"

"She has the story framework. Timed release — the financial exposé hits the wires first, then Helen's piece runs forty-eight hours later with the human accounts. She's been working with Blanche on the testimony structure."
At the name, Bertha's mouth twitched. Not quite a smile. Something closer to respect, reluctantly granted. "Blanche Ingram is going to testify."
"Her words. She said —" Jane hesitated. "She said she'd rather be the one who brought him down than the one he discarded."
"She always was competitive." Bertha returned her attention to the jacket, fingers smoothing a wrinkle in the lining with absent precision. "And Grace?"

Grace spoke from the doorway, her voice flat and certain. "Security feeds are mine. I can loop any camera in the building on thirty seconds' notice. I've been running interference on his monitoring software for months — small glitches, nothing he'd trace. I know which feeds he watches live and which he only reviews when something triggers him."

Jane nodded. The coalition was assembled. Richard with the financial evidence. Helen with the narrative. Blanche with the testimony. Grace with the surveillance infrastructure. Adèle with the access codes to Edward's private server — copied months ago, handed over without trembling. And Jane herself, with the encrypted drive tucked inside a hollowed-out volume of Proust on her bedside shelf.
Which left the most vulnerable piece of all.

"We need to get you out," Jane said. "Before Paris. If Edward discovers what we're building — and he will, the closer we get, the more likely a leak — you're his first target. He'll lock you down, move you somewhere we can't reach you, use you as leverage. We can't let that happen."
Bertha's hands stilled on the fabric. The silence stretched, filled only by the faint hum of the inefficient space heater in the corner and the distant tick of pipes somewhere behind the stone walls.
"No," Bertha said.
"The basement —"
"I'm not leaving."
Jane stared at her. "You just heard me. If he finds out —"

"If I disappear, he'll know something is wrong." Bertha turned on her stool to face Jane fully, and there was something in her expression that stopped the protest forming on Jane's lips. Not defiance. Not the wildness that had defined their early encounters. This was colder. Calculated. "Think about it. I've been here for five years. I don't leave. I don't escape. I don't have visitors. That's the pattern. The moment I break pattern, Edward's paranoia does the rest. He'll postpone the show. Cancel the IPO. Move the money somewhere we can't trace it. And we lose our moment."
"Our moment." Jane's voice was tight. "You're talking about staying in a basement as a strategic asset while a man who has imprisoned you for half a decade could walk through that door at any time."

"I'm talking about winning." Bertha held her gaze without flinching. "He won't find out. Because Grace is going to help me manage him. And you're going to give me access to the security system so I can see what he sees."
"See what he —"
"If I know which cameras are active, which corridors he's monitoring, when he's likely to come downstairs, I can anticipate him. I've been doing it for years, Jane. I just need better tools."

Jane opened her mouth, closed it. The basement air pressed against her skin, cool and close, carrying the faint iron tang of old pipes. She could hear Grace shifting behind her, the creak of the door frame, and beneath it all the soft scratch of Bertha's thumbnail against the worktable.
"That's insane," Jane said finally.

"Probably." Bertha's lips curved, just barely. "But it's also the only way. If I stay, everything looks normal. Edward comes and goes. I work. The show preparation continues. Nothing triggers him. And when we move, we move all at once — no warning, no window for him to react."

Jane pressed her fingers to her temples. The logic was airtight, which made it worse. She could see the shape of the plan, and it terrified her, because it required Bertha to remain exactly where she was — in the jaw of the beast — until the very last moment.
"If I get you access to the security system," Jane said slowly, "and something goes wrong —"
"Then I'll deal with it." Bertha's voice was steady. "I've dealt with worse."

The words landed like stones in still water. Jane looked at her — really looked, past the sharp cheekbones and the ink-dark eyes and the composed stillness that had replaced the feral creature she'd first met in this same room. She saw the architecture beneath. The structure that had been invisible because no one had thought to look for it.
"Fine," Jane said. "You stay. But we need an exit strategy — a real one. Not hope, not improvisation. A plan for getting you out of this basement and into position for Paris."

Bertha was quiet for a long moment. Then she reached beneath the worktable and produced a small box — cardboard, battered, the kind that once held screws or buttons. She set it on the table between them.
"I don't need an extraction," she said. "I need a data extraction."
She opened the box. Inside, nestled in a bed of cotton batting, was a microSD card no larger than a fingernail. It looked insignificant. It looked like nothing.
"What is that?" Jane asked.

"Five years of documentation." Bertha's voice was almost casual, as if she were discussing a fabric order. "Video recordings. Audio. Photographs. Timestamped, catalogued, cross-referenced with Maison Rochester's financial statements and Edward's personal correspondence. Every threat. Every assault. Every stolen design. Every forged signature. Everything he's done to me and everything he's taken from me, recorded and preserved."
Jane's breath caught. "How?"

"I built a camera." Bertha pulled another object from the box — a crude assembly of circuit boards and wire, the cracked lens of a smartphone glued to a housing of soldered tin. "Scavenged electronics from the workshop. A phone lens Grace cracked and smuggled down rather than replacing. I charged it with a defunct sewing machine's power supply. Recorded in thirty-second segments — nothing that would register on the power draw, nothing that would show up on the electrical monitoring Edward installed after the first year."
"You built a camera," Jane repeated. The words didn't seem real.

"I built several. That one is the most recent. The earlier models are disassembled — the components redistributed through the workshop so they look like spare parts." Bertha lifted the microSD card between two fingers, turning it so it caught the lamplight. "It's all here. And it needs to get to Paris."
"Where is it hidden?" Jane reached for the card, but Bertha pulled it back.

"Hidden?" Bertha laughed — a short, surprised sound, as if Jane had asked a question so obvious it bordered on absurd. "It's hidden here." She turned to the punk tuxedo jacket on the table and pressed her thumb against a seam at the interior hem. A tiny slit parted, invisible unless you knew exactly where to look, and Bertha slid the microSD card inside with the practiced ease of someone who had done it a hundred times before. The seam closed, and the card vanished as if it had never existed.
"The jacket," Jane said. "It's in the jacket."

"The jacket that's going to walk down the runway at Paris Fashion Week. In front of every buyer, every journalist, every competitor Edward Rochester has ever known. And when it does, that card goes with it. No one searches a garment on a runway. No one suspects the clothes themselves."

The audacity of it hit Jane like a gust of wind. The punk tuxedo jacket — the piece that had caused such controversy when Edward first presented it, the garment critics had called "savage" and "unmarketable" and "brilliant" — was a Trojan horse. It had always been a Trojan horse.
"How long?" Jane asked. Her voice came out hoarse. "How long have you been —"

"Recording?" Bertha tilted her head. "Since year two. The first year was survival — just survival. I didn't know if I'd live through it. I didn't know if anyone was looking for me. By the second year, I understood that no one was coming. That if I was going to get out, I'd have to build my own door."

Jane stared at her. The basement seemed to contract around them, the walls pressing inward, the low ceiling descending. She could taste the dust on her tongue, feel the chill radiating from the stone floor through the thin soles of her shoes. And beneath it, a sound so faint she might have imagined it — the whisper of fabric against fabric as Bertha's hand rested on the jacket, steady as a pulse.
"Five years," Jane said. "You've been planning this for five years."

Bertha's expression flickered. Something moved behind her eyes — not grief, not pride, but the shadow of an exhaustion so deep it had become geography, a landscape she'd learned to navigate in the dark.
"Planning is a strong word," she said quietly. "I was surviving. The plan came later."
She stood, and the jacket came with her, lifted from the table with a care that bordered on reverence. She turned it inside out in one fluid motion, and Jane's breath stopped.
The lining was a map.

Not a metaphor. A literal, painstaking, impossibly detailed schematic rendered in silk thread of varying colors — blue for water lines and plumbing, red for the security system's camera positions and sensor fields, green for the server architecture she'd somehow mapped from below, gold for the structural elements of the château itself. Every corridor, every door, every blind spot. Every camera, its field of vision marked with tiny precise stitches that fanned outward like lashes. The basement — her basement — rendered in such minute detail that Jane could see the exact path from the worktable to the utility duct, the one that bypassed three of the four security cameras and emerged in the service corridor behind the kitchens.
"My God," Jane whispered.

She leaned closer, fingers hovering over the silk. The stitching was immaculate — smaller than any stitch she'd ever seen, almost microscopic. How many hours? How many years of working by lamplight, sewing her recon into the very garment Edward would claim as his own?

"The security system," Bertha said, pointing to the red threads. "I reverse-engineered it in year three. Grace helped — she'd tell me when he changed the passcodes, and I'd work out the algorithm. It's a rotating six-digit cipher based on the date and a seed number he thinks no one knows. I know it." Her finger traced a path through the red threads, following a route that wound through camera blind spots like a river through stones. "Every flaw. Every back door. Every gap in coverage. He's had the system upgraded twice — I mapped it both times within a week."
"You reverse-engineered the security system. From a basement. With scavenged parts."
"I had time." Bertha's voice was dry as kindling. "And motivation."

Jane's gaze moved across the silk map, cataloguing details. The server positions, marked in green. The electrical junction boxes. The places where the walls were thinnest, where a determined person might — "The HELP messages," she said suddenly, looking up. "The seams. The garment seams that Adèle found in the archives — the coded distress signal in the stitching pattern. That was you."

"Of course it was me." Bertha angled the jacket so the seam work near the collar caught the light — and there it was, the pattern Jane had first noticed weeks ago. The irregular stitch spacing that wasn't irregular at all, but code. "I needed two things from that signal. First, that someone might find it and understand. A distress call. Second — I needed it to be a breadcrumb trail. If anyone ever investigated the garments, if anyone looked at Maison Rochester's archive with suspicious eyes, the seams would lead them here. To me. To this room. To the truth."

She turned the jacket right-side out again, and the map vanished as though it had never been, replaced by the sleek black exterior with its radical cuts and deconstructed lapels. The punk jacket. The impossible garment that had made Edward Rochester's reputation.
Jane looked at Bertha Mason — at the woman who had been erased, imprisoned, dismissed as mad — and felt something shift in her chest. Not pity. Something closer to awe.
"You're a genius," she said.
Bertha's face didn't change, but something hardened behind her eyes. "Edward certainly thought so," she said. "He just put his name on it."
The words hung between them. Jane thought of the encrypted drive, the shell companies Richard had traced, the testimony Blanche was preparing, the timed detonation Helen would trigger with her story.
None of it was bigger than this room. None of it was bigger than the woman sitting in front of her, who had turned five years of captivity into a weapon and stitched it into silk.
"Three weeks," Jane said. "Paris Fashion Week is in three weeks. We need a timeline."
Bertha nodded. "I stay until three days before the show. Then Grace triggers the extraction."
"Grace?"

The woman spoke from the doorway, her arms crossed, her expression unchanged. "I call a medical emergency. Bertha collapses — I'm the only one authorized to handle it. I take her out through the service corridor" — she gestured toward the map Bertha had just concealed — "through the blind spot behind the east wing kitchens. I drive her to the clinic in Amboise, except we don't stop in Amboise. We keep going. Richard will have a safe house in Paris."
"What about the security feeds?" Jane asked.

"Looped. I've been pre-rendering footage for months — standard segments of Bertha working, sleeping, eating. Boring, repetitive, exactly what Edward expects to see if he checks. I splice it in, he watches a woman who looks like she's having a quiet night. By the time the loop breaks, it won't matter."
"And Edward?"

"Edward thinks Bertha is in a private clinic," Bertha said. "Grace will tell him I had an episode — mental health crisis. He'll believe it. He always believes the narrative that makes me look broken. He'll instruct Grace to handle it quietly. He'll wait. He'll expect me back in a few days."
"And by the time he realizes you're not coming back," Jane said slowly, "the show will have already started."
Bertha smiled. It was not a kind smile, but it was the first genuine one Jane had seen on her face — the smile of someone who had waited a very long time for a debt to come due.

"The punk jacket walks the runway," Bertha said. "With me in the audience, with Richard at the door, with Helen's story already filed and Blanche on record. And inside the jacket — inside the seams that Edward never bothered to look at closely, because why would he? He doesn't sew. He doesn't create. He takes — inside those seams is every piece of evidence needed to destroy him."

She laid the jacket flat on the worktable again, smoothing the lapels with both hands. The lamplight fell across the black fabric, catching the sharp angles of the cut, the deliberate deconstruction that had scandalized the fashion world. A garment built for war.

"I'll need access to the security system by Thursday," Bertha said. "Grace can show you how to open the back door — it's the one thing I can't do from down here. I need to see what he sees. I need to know when he's moving, where he's looking, whether he's starting to suspect anything."
"I'll have it for you by Wednesday," Jane said.

"Good." Bertha picked up her shears again, returning to the seam she'd been trimming. The transition was seamless — from strategist to seamstress, as though the conversation had been just another task in a day full of them.
Jane turned to leave, her hand on the door frame, Grace's bulk behind her like a wall. She paused.
"Bertha."
The shears paused. "Yes?"
"Thank you. For trusting me with this."

Bertha looked at her — really looked, the way she hadn't before, the way she might look at a piece of fabric to determine its thread count, its tensile strength, its capacity to hold under pressure. Measuring.
"I'm not trusting you with my life," Bertha said. "I'm trusting you with my evidence. My life, I've been handling on my own for quite some time."
It was not unkind. It was simply true. Jane nodded once and climbed the stairs, the weight of the basement settling behind her like a door closing.

Above her, the château hummed with its usual rhythms — footsteps on marble, the distant clatter of the kitchen, Edward's voice carrying from his office in a tone that suggested another phone call with investors. The ordinary sounds of a house built on theft.

Somewhere in that house, an encrypted drive waited in a hollowed book. Somewhere in that house, a teenage girl carried access codes in her memory like a hiding place. Somewhere in that house, a network of conspirators moved through the rooms with careful, ordinary faces.

And below, in the basement, a woman with silver shears and five years of patience sat at her worktable, sewing the end of Edward Rochester into the lining of a jacket that the whole world was waiting to see.

Three weeks. The punk jacket would walk. The seams would speak. And Bertha Mason — who had been erased, rewritten, and dismissed as mad — would step into the light he had stolen from her and take it all back.
